post: Control who a people can trade with, and you control how they live. That's what made Grievance 16 more than an economic complaint. The Navigation Acts, first passed in the 1600s, were designed to keep the colonies tightly bound to the British economy. For most of their existence, they weren't strictly enforced, colonists traded broadly, built prosperous industries in timber and tobacco, and developed commercial relationships across the Atlantic. When Britain began enforcing these laws aggressively in the years before the Revolution, and then closed the port of Boston entirely in 1774, it wasn't experienced as stricter regulation. It was experienced as the sudden withdrawal of a freedom that had defined colonial life for generations. Grace Richter of George Washington University frames the colonists' response precisely: this was a natural rights argument, not merely a commercial one. The ability to trade freely, to determine one's own economic future, was inseparable from the liberty the colonists believed they were owed. Episode 16 of our 27-part series is live. Join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #NavigationActs

Tecnau Inc. is proud to support the important work of the Bill of Rights Institute in bringing civics education to life for students across the U. S. Recently, we received a series of thoughtful thank-you messages from educators who received classroom copies of these materials, and the impact is remarkable. Justice requires accountability. Accountability requires consequences. Consequences require a trial, and a trial held somewhere else is a trial designed not to convict. That's the colonists' argument in Grievance 15. The Administration of Justice Act of 1774 allowed cases involving British soldiers to be moved outside Massachusetts when a fair trial supposedly couldn't be guaranteed. John Hancock called the soldiers it protected "blood suckers." Patriots called the Act a license to act without fear of punishment. The historical irony, as Hannah Nolan of the University of Maryland explains, is that colonial courts had already demonstrated they could be fair: the Boston Massacre trials resulted in acquittals and reduced sentences, not the vengeful convictions public outrage might have produced. The Crown's stated justification for moving trials  protecting fairness  was contradicted by the evidence already on the record. When those in power can move the arena of accountability to wherever it suits them, the rule of law stops protecting everyone equally. That was the colonists' fear in 1776. It's still a live question today. Episode 15 of our 27-part series is live. Join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #RuleOfLaw  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #USHistory

Civics is supposed to be the subject that teaches Americans how to disagree well. So why are so many civics teachers afraid to teach it? Karissa Waddick with USA TODAY explored that question this week, and the answers are uncomfortable. An iCivics survey found more than half of teachers report fearing backlash for covering something the "wrong way." Basic civics concepts, the kind that have been taught in American classrooms for generations, are now described as difficult to teach. BRI President David Bobb is quoted in the piece, reflecting on what a decade of deepening political divisions has done to the classroom, and what it will take to restore civic education to what it's supposed to be: not partisan, not ideological, but grounded in the founding principles and primary sources that belong to every American student. It's a worthwhile read. Especially right now, 29 days before the nation's 250th birthday:  https://bit.ly/4uhlwBw  #CivicsEducation  #USAToday  #iCivics

In 2025, BRI ran 315 professional development programs across 27 states and Washington, D. C., reaching 8,099 teachers. The programs we're most proud of aren't the ones with the highest single-day turnout. They're the ones where we're still showing up years later. Our PD is built around the Founding documents  the Declaration, the Constitution, the Bill of Rights  and the civic principles that come up in every election, every courtroom, and every classroom debate. We design programming to compound: each session builds on the last, and each year gives teachers a deeper foundation to teach from. With America's 250th here in just a few weeks, there's no better moment to build something that lasts beyond it. What would your district's civics program look like with a PD partner for the next 3 years, not just the next 3 weeks? 🔗 Request a program:  https://bit.ly/4fZoZ46  #CivicsEducation  #ProfessionalDevelopment  #AmericanHistory  #K12Education  #America250

The Third Amendment is the least-litigated provision in the Bill of Rights. It exists because of Grievance 14. The Quartering Acts of 1765 and 1774 required colonists to provide food and shelter, at their own expense, for British troops stationed in the colonies. Parliament specified uninhabited buildings, not private homes. But the Founders understood that the line between "your barn" and "your house" was less important than the principle: a government that can compel citizens to support a military presence they never consented to has compromised the very liberty it exists to protect. The Third Amendment's language reflects that understanding precisely: no soldier quartered in any house without the owner's consent in peacetime, nor in wartime except as the law prescribes. The amendment has rarely been tested in court because the constitutional protection it enshrined removed the conditions that made the grievance possible. C. David Carlson of Trinity Academy South Bend explains the history behind both in Episode 14 of our 27-part series. Join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/4fnlTXp  #ThirdAmendment  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #USHistory

When two sides disagree about who has the authority to make the rules, there is no compromise available, only a decision about who wins. That's the situation Grievance 13 describes. Parliament passed the Stamp Act, the Sugar Act, and the Intolerable Acts believing it was acting within its constitutional rights. Colonists believed Parliament was overstepping, imposing laws and taxes on people who had no voice in the body making them. Both sides had arguments. Neither was willing to yield on the underlying question of legitimate authority. And as Jennifer Seiter of the UVA Democracy and Capitalism Lab explains, as long as the colonies remained under parliamentary power, that question could never be resolved in the colonists' favor. Independence wasn't a last resort. By 1776, it was the only answer to an impasse that couldn't be negotiated away. Episode 13 of our 27-part series is live. Join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #NoTaxationWithoutRepresentation

In 1774, one man held both the governorship of Massachusetts and the command of all British forces in North America. Neither role was elected. Both were appointed by the king. That consolidation is Grievance 12 of the Declaration of Independence. Thomas Gage's dual appointment as royal governor and Commander in Chief fused civil and military authority into a single unaccountable office, with no mechanism for the colonists to check either one. The Enlightenment thinkers the Founders relied on had been clear on this point: separating sites of power is the structural guarantee against abuse. Checks and balances don't work when the same person holds all the levers. The U. S. Constitution reflects exactly that lesson. The president needs congressional approval to declare war. The executive cannot deploy military force at will. Those constraints exist in direct response to what Grievance 12 describes: what happens when civil and military power answer to the same hand. BRI's Dr. C. C. Borzilleri breaks it down in Episode 12 of our 27-part series. Discover more and join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M New episodes every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday.  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #SeparationOfPowers
